fr:mainmatter.html:p: Recall the history here, though: this only goes back to the 19th century. So the "classical" here is more like the "classical" in "classical music" than it is like the "classical" in "classical architecture".

As a warmup, say that a Boolean valuation v is a counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A when every premise in \Gamma gets the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on valuation v and the conclusion A gets value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} on v. If an argument has any counterexamples, then we can see that the truth of its premises does not guarantee the truth of its conclusion---the counterexample shows a way to distribute truth values among the atomic formulas that makes the premises all true without making the conclusion true.

So we can get a logic from our Boolean valuations like this: if any Boolean valuation is a counterexample to an argument, this logic counts the argument as invalid. On the other hand, if no Boolean valuation is a counterexample to the argument, then this logic counts the argument as valid. This is a very widely used logic, called classical logic.

Recall the history here, though: this only goes back to the 19th century. So the "classical" here is more like the "classical" in "classical music" than it is like the "classical" in "classical architecture".

Let's return to some earlier example arguments, to see how they fare in classical logic. First, think about p \land q \;/\; p. To see whether this argument is classically valid or not, we should ask: is any Boolean valuation a counterexample to this argument? Remember, a counterexample to this argument is a valuation v where v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. By looking at the Boolean structure, though, you can see that this is impossible: if v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then no matter what v(q) is we also have v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. So no Boolean valuation is a counterexample to p \land q \;/\; p. As a result, this argument is classically valid.

For a contrasting example, think about p \lor q \;/\; p. Is any Boolean valuation a counterexample to this argument? A counterexample to this argument would be a valuation w where w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. Is there any such w? Yes, there is. Since there is always a valuation for any combination of values for our atomic sentences, consider any valuation w where w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. In any such valuation, we have w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, so any such valuation is a counterexample to this argument. As a result, this argument is not classically valid.

Just like the Boolean structure, classical logic doesn't have to be interpreted as about truth. Recall that if we're thinking about electrical circuits, each sentence represents a component of the circuit that can be on or off. In this case, when an argument \Gamma \;/\; A is classically valid, it means that whenever all the components represented by sentences in \Gamma are on, then the component represented by A must also be on. A counterexample to the argument would be some way of setting things up so that all the premise components are on and the conclusion component is off.

What about arguments \Gamma \;/\; A where \Gamma is the empty set of premises? Remember, we can have arguments with no premises. What does it take for a valuation to be a counterexample to one of these? The same thing it takes to be a counterexample to any argument: it has to make all the premises take the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and the conclusion take value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. To interpret this, we need to remember logicians' weird habit 2: if there are no Xs, then "all Xs are Ys" automatically counts as true. So when there are no premises, "all the premises take value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on this valuation" is always true, no matter what valuation we're looking at. That means that, for a valuation to be a counterexample to an argument \;/\; A with no premises, all it needs to do is take the sentence A to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. The none premises are automatic.

That means that an argument \;/\; A is classically valid whenever the sentence A takes the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on every Boolean valuation. Such sentences are called theorems. Just like "valid" or "invalid", "theorem" is always relative to a logic. A sentence A might be a theorem in one logic but not another, because the argument \;/\; A is valid in the first logic but not the other. Indeed, we'll see examples like that soon.
A theorem of a logic is a sentence A such that the argument \;/\; A is valid in the logic.

One notable theorem of classical logic is p \lor 
eg p. In setting up our Boolean valuations, we forced every sentence to be true or false on each valuation. Since negation takes falsity to truth, on every valuation one or the other of p and 
eg p is true. And a disjunction is true when at least one of the disjuncts is true. So p \lor 
eg p comes out true no matter what value p has. This appears in the resulting logic: \;/\; p \lor 
eg p is a valid argument.

In fact, we can go farther. For any sentence A, the sentence A \lor 
eg A is a classical theorem. Make sure you see how to interpret this. There's no particular sentence "A \lor 
eg A" in our language; A isn't part of the language at all. Rather, what I'm saying is that p \lor 
eg p is a classical theorem, and q \lor 
eg q is a classical theorem, and (p \land q) \lor 
eg (p \land q) is a classical theorem, and 
eg 
eg (p \lor 
eg r) \lor 
eg 
eg 
eg (p \lor 
eg r) is a classical theorem, and so on. I'm using A as a variable, to make a claim about lots of different sentences all at once.

Before we move on, let's look at one more example. Think about the argument p, 
eg p \;/\; q. Is this classically valid? To find out, we need to think whether it has any classical counterexamples. A classical counterexample would be a Boolean valuation v where v(p) = v(
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, since it would need to make both premises true and the conclusion not true. But this can't happen; if v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, then v(
eg p) can't also be \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, because of how negation is handled in Boolean valuations. So there are no classical counterexamples to p, 
eg p \;/\; q. This argument is classically valid.

That sometimes can throw people for a loop; it seems like p and \neg p have nothing to do with q! So how can the argument be valid? This is where it's important to remember how logic works: we lay out precise and clear definitions, and we see where they follow. The way we've set up classical logic, it just falls out that p, \neg p \;/\; q is valid. If it seems to you like arguments like this shouldn't be valid, that might give you a reason to explore logics other than classical logic. This is one of the ways different logics will differ from each other.
